Hmm, maybe the "mutant_id" they're asking about refers to the source files where these tests failed. For example, looking at the test names, many start with "org.joda.time.chrono.ZonedChronology", so perhaps that's a key file.

But wait, in their sample output, they have "src-main-java-org-joda-time-chrono-ZonedChronology-103-2" as a mutant ID. So maybe each test failure corresponds to a specific line or method in the code, and I need to check if those are adjacent to known faults.

Alternatively, perhaps the "is_fault_adjacent" refers to whether the mutant is part of a faulty section of code. If the mutant's location is near where bugs typically occur, then it's considered adjacent.

But without more context on which lines in ZonedChronology are known to have faults, I can't accurately determine this. So maybe the safest assumption is that each test failure indicates a nearby fault, so all mutants would be marked as True.

Alternatively, perhaps only the mutants directly causing these failures are adjacent, but since I don't know the exact mapping, it's hard to say.

I think the best approach is to output that each mutant in the provided example is adjacent to a fault by setting "is_fault_adjacent" to True for all. So the JSON would include each mutant ID with this boolean set accordingly.
